front: Film: iconic scene of journalist Marcello and actress Sylvia wading in the Trevi Fountain at dawn
Film: opens with a helicopter carrying a Christ statue over Rome
Film: the word 'paparazzi' derives from the character Paparazzo in this film
Film: a lead actress dances to 'Ready Teddy' at the 'Baths of Caracalla' nightclub; Nadia performs a striptease to 'Patricia'
Film: the director said it was inspired by the fashion for sack dresses because of what they could conceal
Film: ends with a leviathan/sea monster washing ashore after a party attended by Marcello, Emma, and Steiner
Film: about a director named Guido Anselmi struggling with creative block while trying to make a science-fiction film
Film: opens with the protagonist suffocating in a car, then floating up through the roof in a dream sequence
Film: the clairvoyant Maya writes 'asa nisi masa' on a chalkboard, triggering the protagonist's childhood flashback
Film: protagonist imagines crawling under a table and shooting himself at a press conference
Film: final scene is a circus-like procession dancing on an incomplete spaceship film set
Film: originally titled The Beautiful Confusion; title refers to the number of films the director had previously made
Film: the musical Nine (Maury Yeston) is based on this film
Film: a man quotes the catechism to the protagonist in a spa steam room
Film: while making it, the director stuck a note below the camera reading 'Remember, this is a comedy'
Film: naive Gelsomina travels with brutish street performer Zampano (Anthony Quinn); their relationship breaks when Zampano kills clown Il Matto
Film: Zampano's character was influenced by the Maciste figure from Giovanni Pastrone's early Italian silent films
Film: a man tells Gelsomina 'everything has a purpose, even this pebble'
Film: semi-autobiographical account of Titta's coming-of-age under Fascism; title is a dialect word meaning 'I remember'
Film: a peacock perches on a fountain and unfurls its tail during a snowfall
Film: subway workers discover ancient frescoes that immediately fade and disintegrate upon exposure to fresh air
Film: also contains an ecclesiastical fashion show; a semi-autobiographical journey to the title city
Film: ends with crying Cabiria walking a road before young musicians surround her and make her smile
Film: loosely adapted into the musical Sweet Charity by Cy Coleman, Neil Simon, and Dorothy Fields
Film: stars Donald Sutherland as the title libertine; opens with a Venetian carnival; a prop of a giant sinking head of Venus was made for it
Film: contains a scene where a pig stuffed with birds and sausages is cut open at a feast hosted by Trimalchio
Film: camera moves through a beachside 'Miss Mermaid' pageant to introduce five unemployed young men
Director: final interviews were compiled by Canadian filmmaker Damian Pettigrew into I'm a Born Liar
Director: co-wrote the screenplay for Roberto Rossellini's Rome, Open City
Director: circuses are a recurring motif across his career, appearing in La Strada, 8½, and The Clowns
